children.name: Pomatostomus
children.description: The Australasian babblers — a genus of medium-sized, strongly social passerines with stout, decurved bills and long, broad tails held cocked at an angle. They live in closely bonded family groups of up to twenty birds that cooperate to build large, domed stick nests, defend territories, and rear young. Their rich, chattering calls are a characteristic sound of the Australian bush.

children.name: Pomatostomus temporalis
children.description: The largest Australasian babbler, with a distinctive white-tipped tail and a rich, musical, chattering song that echoes through woodlands across northern and eastern Australia and Papua New Guinea. It lives in permanent family groups that build large, communal roosting nests of sticks in the forks of trees. Its cooperative breeding system involves all group members feeding the young.

children.name: Pomatostomus superciliosus
children.description: A medium-sized babbler with a bold white eyebrow, a brown crown, and a heavily streaked white throat, found across southern Australia. It is a highly social species that lives in noisy, chattering groups of five to fifteen birds, constantly calling to one another as they forage on the ground. It builds elaborate domed nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Pomatostomus ruficeps
children.description: A striking babbler with a rich chestnut crown and nape, a white throat, and a long, curved bill, endemic to the arid and semi-arid interior of southeastern Australia. It lives in small, tight-knit groups that defend large territories in dry eucalypt woodlands and mallee. Its complex social structure makes it a key study species for understanding cooperative breeding.

children.name: Pomatostomus halli
children.description: A medium-sized babbler with a pale brown crown, white throat, and dark brown upperparts, found in dry eucalypt woodlands and acacia scrub of eastern Australia. It lives in small family groups that cooperate to build domed stick nests. Named after the Australian ornithologist Ian Hall.

children.name: Pomatostomus isidorei
children.description: Found in the lowland forests of New Guinea, this babbler has a dark brown body, a white throat, and a long, curved bill. It is the only Australasian babbler found outside Australia, inhabiting the southern lowlands of Papua New Guinea. It lives in small, noisy groups that forage on the forest floor for insects.
